concepts.title: Angular Momentum of a particle about some  point
concepts.conceptBody:
Angular momentum of a particle about some point 

Suppose a particle A of mass $\text{m}$ is moving with linear momentum $\vec{P}=\text{m}\vec{v}$. Its angular momentum

$\left( {\vec{L}} \right)$ about point $\text{O}$ is defined as :

$\vec{L}=\vec{r}\times \vec{P}=\vec{r}\times \left( \text{m}\vec{v} \right)=\text{m}\left( \vec{r}\times \vec{v} \right)$

Here, $\vec{r}$ is the radius vector of particle $\text{A}$ about $\text{O}$ at the instant of time.

The magnitude of is $\vec{L}$ is $\text{L}=\text{mvr sin }\theta =\text{mv}{{\text{r}}_{\bot }}$


Here, ${{r}_{\bot }}=r\text{sin}\theta $ is the perpendicular distance of line of action of velocity $\vec{v}$ from point $O$. The direction of $\vec{L}$ is same as that of $\vec{r}\times \vec{V}$

Note : The angular momentum of a particle about a line (say $\text{AB}$ ) is the component along $\text{AB}$ of the angular momentum of the particle about any point (say $\text{O}$ ) on the line $\text{AB}$. This component is independent of the choice of point $O$, so far as it is chosen on the line $AB$.

Example

A particle of mass $m$ is projected from origin $O$ with speed $\text{u}$ at an angle $\theta $ with positive $\text{x}$-axis. Positive $\text{y}$-axis is in vertically upward direction. Find the angular momentum of particle at any time $t$ about $O$ before the particle strikes the ground again.

Solution : 

$\vec{L}=\text{m}\left( \vec{r}\times \vec{v} \right)$

\(\begin{aligned} & \text { Here } \vec{r}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{xi}+\mathrm{yj}=(\mathrm{u} \cos \theta) t \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\left(\mathrm{ut} \sin \theta-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^2\right) \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \text { and } \vec{v}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{i}+\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{j}=(\mathrm{u} \cos \theta) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(\mathrm{u} \sin \theta-\mathrm{gt}) \hat{\mathrm{j}}\end{aligned}\)

\(\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{m}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}})=\mathrm{m}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ (\mathrm{u} \cos \theta) & (\mathrm{u} \sin \theta) \mathrm{t}-\frac{1}{2} g t^2 & 0 \\ \mathrm{u} \cos \theta & \mathrm{u} \sin \theta & 0\end{array}\right|\)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=m\left[ \left( {{u}^{2}}\text{ sin}\theta \text{ cos}\theta \right)t-\left( u\text{ cos}\theta \right)g{{t}^{2}}-\left( {{u}^{2}}\text{sin}\theta \text{cos}\theta \right)t+\frac{1}{2}\left( u\text{ cos}\theta \right)g{{t}^{2}} \right]\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \\ {} & ~=-\frac{1}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}\left( \text{ucos}\theta \right)g{{\text{t}}^{2}}\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\, \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Angular momentum of a rigid body
concepts.conceptBody:
Angular momentum of a rigid body (or a system of particle) 

Rigid body : A rigid body is a special case of system of particles where in the distance between the constituent particles remain fixed.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{{\vec{r}}}_{\text{i}}}=\left( \vec{r}_{1}^{'}+{{{\vec{r}}}_{\text{CM}}} \right) \\ {{{\vec{v}}}_{\text{i}}}=\left( \vec{v}_{1}^{'}+{{{\vec{v}}}_{\text{CM}}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

Where ${{\vec{r}}_{\text{i}}}$ and ${{\vec{v}}_{\text{i}}}$ are position vector and velocity vector o the particle with respect to origin and $\vec{r}_{\text{i}}^{'}$ and $\vec{v}_{\text{i}}^{'}$ are of the particle with respect to the centre of mass of body

Let us calculate the angular momentum of the given rigid body (system of particle) about a point $\text{O}$.

\(\vec{L}_0=\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left(\vec{r}_{\mathrm{i}} \times \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{i}} \vec{v}_{\mathrm{i}}\right)\)
$\begin{array}{l} {{{\vec{L}}}_{0}} =\sum \left[ \left( \vec{r}_{\text{i}}^{'}+{{\text{r}}_{\text{CM}}} \right)\times {{\text{m}}_{\text{i}}}\left( \vec{v}_{\text{i}}^{'}+{{{\vec{v}}}_{\text{CM}}} \right) \right] \\ =\left[ \sum \left( \vec{r}_{\text{i}}^{'}\times {{\text{m}}_{\text{i}}}\vec{v}_{\text{i}}^{'} \right) \right]+\left[ {{{\vec{r}}}_{\text{CM}}}\times \left( \sum {{\text{m}}_{\text{i}}} \right){{\text{v}}_{\text{CM}}} \right] \\ =\sum \vec{r}_{\text{i}}^{'}\times {{\text{m}}_{\text{i}}}\vec{v}_{\text{i}}^{'}+\left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{\text{CM}}}\times {{\text{M}}_{\text{CM}}} \right) \\ {{{\vec{L}}}_{0}} ={{{\vec{L}}}_{\text{CM}}}+{{{\vec{L}}}_{\text{CM}}},0 \\ \end{array}$

Thus, (angular momentum of a system with respect to $\text{O}$ ) $=($ angular momentum of particles w.r.t. CM $)+($ angular momentum of CM w.r.t. O) Also L $\left( \text{z} \right)={{\text{L}}_{\text{CM}}}\left( \text{z} \right)+\left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{\text{CM}}}\times {{\text{M}}_{\text{CM}}} \right)\cdot \overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\,$

$\begin{array}{l} \text{ angular }\text{ momentum } \\ \text{ of }\text{ a }\text{ system }\text{ with } \\ \text{ respect }\text{ to }\text{ an }\text{ axis } \\ \end{array}=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ angular }\text{ momentum }\text{ of } \\ \text{ system }\text{ of }\text{ particle }\text{ with } \\ \text{ respect }\text{ to }\text{ CM }\text{ along }\text{ an } \\ \text{ arc }\text{ parallel }\text{ to }\text{ given }\text{ axis } \\ \text{ and }\text{ passing }\text{ through }\text{ CM } \\ \end{array} \right\}+\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ angular }\text{ momentum }\text{ o } \\ \text{ the }\text{ CM }\text{ along }\text{ that } \\ \text{ axis } \\ \end{array} \right\}$

concepts.title: Conservation of angular momentum
concepts.conceptBody:
Conservation of Angular Momentum 

Net angular momentum of a system of particles about an axis remains conserved, if net external torque on the system is zero about the same axis. The angular momentum of a particle about some reference point $\{\mathrm{O}\}$ is defined as

\(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}\) ...(i)

Here $\vec{P}$ is the linear momentum of the particle and $\vec{r}$ its position vector with respect to the reference point $O$. Differentiating Eq. (i) with respect to time we get,

\(\frac{\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{r}} \times \frac{\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{P}}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\frac{\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{r}}}{\mathrm{dt}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{P}}\) ...(ii)
Here \(\frac{d \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}\)
and \(\frac{d \vec{r}}{d t}=\vec{v}\) (velocity of particle)

Hence, Eq. (ii) can be rewritten as, $\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} + \vec{v} \times \vec{P}$ Now, $\vec{v} \times \vec{P} = 0$, because $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{P}$ are parallel to each other and the cross product of two parallel vectors is zero. Thus, \( \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} = \vec{\tau} \)

or \(\vec{\tau}=\frac{d \vec{L}}{d t}\) ...(iii)

Which states that the time rate of change of angular momentum of a particle about some reference point in an inertial frame of reference is equal to the net torques acting on it. This result is the rotational analog of the equation $\vec{F} = \frac{d\vec{P}}{dt}$, which states that the time rate of change of the linear momentum of a particle is equal to the force acting on it. Eq. (iii), like all vector equations, is equivalent to three scalar equations, namely

\(\tau_{x} = \frac{dL}{dt}_{x}, \quad \tau_{y} = \frac{dL}{dt}_{y}, \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_{z} = \frac{dL}{dt}_{z}\)

The same equation can be generalized for a system of particles as, $ \vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$. According to which the time rate of change of the total angular momentum of a system of particles about some reference point of an inertial frame of reference is equal to the sum of all external torques (of course the vector sum) acting on the system about the same reference point.

Now, suppose that $ \vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = 0$, then $\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = 0$, so that $\vec{L}$ is constant.

"When the resultant external torque acting on a system is zero, the total vector angular momentum of the system remains constant. This is the principle of the conservation of angular momentum. For a rigid body rotating about an axis (the z-axis, say) that is fixed in an inertial reference frame, we have

\[ L_{z} = I\omega \]

It is possible for the moment of inertia $I$ of a rotating body to change by rearrangement of its parts. If no net external torque acts, then $L_{z}$ must remain constant and if $I$ does change, there must be a compensating change in $\omega$. The principle of conservation of angular momentum in this case is expressed as

\[ I\omega = \text{constant} \]

Points to remember

  1. In law of motion for a single particle acted on by a torque $\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$
  2. The law of motion for a system of particles acted on by a resultant external torque
  • Suppose a rod is lying on a smooth horizontal surface when a particle strikes it at some point. Then the angular momentum of the rod + particle remains conserved about any point fixed to an inertial frame of reference before and after the collision as $\vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = 0$, about any point.
  • Suppose a rod is suspended from a support at $O$ and a particle strikes the rod at some point, then the angular momentum of the rod + particle remains conserved only about the point of suspension or point $O$. Because in this case $\vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}}$ on the system is zero only about $O$.

concepts.title: Toppling and angular impulse
concepts.conceptBody:
Toppling

Suppose a force $\text{F}$ is applied at a height $\text{b}$ above the base $\text{AE}$ of the block. Further, suppose the friction $\text{f}$ is sufficient to prevent sliding. In this case, if the normal reaction $\text{N}$ also passed through $\text{C}$, then despite the fact that the block is in translational equilibrium $(\text{F}=$ $\text{f}$ and $\text{N}=\text{mg}$ ), an unbalanced torque (due to the couple of forces $\text{F}$ and $\text{f}$ ) is there. This torque has a tendency to topple the block about point $\text{E}$. To cancel the effect of this unbalanced torque the normal reaction $\text{N}$ is shifted towards right a distance ' $a$ ' such that, net anticlockwise torque is equal to the net clockwise torque or

$\text{F}.\text{b}=\left( \text{mg} \right)\text{a}$

or

$\text{a}=\frac{\text{Fb}}{\text{mg}}$

Now, as F or b (or both) are increased, distance a also increases. But it can not go beyond the right edge of the block. So in extreme case (beyond which the block will topple down), the normal reaction passes through $\text{E}$ as shown in Fig.(b).

Now, if $\text{F}$ or $\text{b}$ are further increased, the block will topple down. This is why the block having the broader base has less chances of toppling in comparison to a block of smaller base. Because the block of larger base has more margin for the normal reaction to shift. On the similar ground we can see why the rolling is so easy.

Because in this case the normal reaction has zero margin to shift. So even if the body is in translational equilibrium ( $\text{F}=\text{f},\text{N}=\text{mg}$ ) an unbalanced torque is left behind and the body starts rolling clockwise.


As soon as the body starts rolling the force of friction is so adjusted (both in magnitude and direction) that either the pure rolling starts (if friction is sufficient enough) or the body starts sliding. Let us take few example related to toppling.

Example

A uniform cube of side ' $a$ ' and mass $m$ rests on a rough horizontal table. A horizontal force $F$ is applied normal to one of the faces at a point directly above the centre of the face, at a height $\frac{3a}{4}$ above the base. What is the minimum value of $F$ for which the cube begins to tip about an edge ? 

Solution : 

In the limiting case normal reaction will pass through $\text{O}$. The cube will tip about $\text{O}$ if torque of $\text{F}$ exceeds the torque of $\text{mg}$.

Hence, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}\left( \frac{3\text{a}}{4} \right)>\text{mg}\left( \frac{\text{a}}{2} \right)$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}>\frac{2}{3}\text{mg}$

Therefore, minimum value of $\text{F}$ is $\frac{2}{3}\text{mg}$

Example

A uniform cylinder of height $h$ and radius $r$ is placed with its circular face on a rough inclined plane and the inclination of the plane to the horizontal is gradually increased. If $\mu $ is the coefficient of friction, then under what conditions the cylinder will (a) slide before toppling (b) topple before sliding. 

Solution : 

(a) The cylinder will slide if $\text{mgsin}\theta >\mu \text{mgcos}\theta $

or

The cylinder will topple if $\left( \text{mg sin }\theta \right)\frac{\text{h}}{2}>\left( \text{mg cos }\theta \right)\text{r}$

Or

Thus, the condition of sliding is $\text{tan}\theta >\mu $ and condition of toppling is $\text{tan}\theta >\frac{2\text{r}}{\text{h}}$. Hence, the cylinder will slide before toppling if

$\mu <\frac{2\text{r}}{\text{h}}$ Ans. 

(b) The cylinder will topple before sliding if $\mu >\frac{2r}{h}$ Ans.

examples.title: A particle of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is on a smooth horizontal table and moves in a circular path of radius $0.6 \mathrm{~m}$. The height of the table from the ground is 0.8 $\mathrm{m}$. If the angular speed of the particle is $12 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ the magnitude of its angular momentum about a point on the ground right under the centre of the circle is
If the angular momentum of a particle of mass $m$ rotating along a circular path of radius $r$ with uniform speed is $L$, the centripetal force acting on the particle is

Two discs are rotating about their axes, normal to the plane of the discs and passing through the centre of the discs. Disc $D$ has $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass and $0.2 \mathrm{~m}$ radius and initial angular velocity of $50 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. Disc $D_2$ has $4 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass, $0.1 \mathrm{~m}$ radius and initial angular velocity of $200 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The two discs are brought in contact face to face with their axes of rotation coincident. The final angular velocity (in rad/s) of the system is

A child with mass $m$ is standing at the edge of a playground merry-go-round (A large uniform disc which rotates in horizontal plane about a fixed vertical axis in parks) with moment of inertia Vs I, radius R, and initial angular velocity w as shown in the figure. The child jumps off the edge of the merry-go-round with a velocity $\mathrm{v}$ with respect to the ground in direction tangent to periphery of the disc as shown. The new angular veloity of the merry-go-round is:

A bullet of mass $10 \mathrm{~g}$ and speed $500 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is fired into a door and gets embedded exactly at the centre of the door. The door is $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ wide and weighs $12 \mathrm{~kg}$. It is hinged at one end and rotates about a vertical axis practically without friction. The angular speed of the door just after the bullet embeds into it will be:

A horizontal turn table in the form of a disc of radius $\mathrm{r}$ carries a gun at $\mathrm{G}$ and rotates with angular velocity $\omega_0$ about a vertical axis passing through the centre $O$. The increase in angular velocity of the system if the gun fires a bullet of mass $\mathrm{m}$ with a tangential velocity $\mathrm{v}$ with respect to the gun is (moment of inertia of gun+table about $\mathrm{O}$ is $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{o}}$ )
A particle is moving along a line $y=x+a \mid$ with a constant velocity $v$. Find the angular momentum of the particle about the origin.

A particle of mass $20 \mathrm{~g}$ is released with an initial velocity $5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ along the curve from the point $A$, as shown in the figure. The point $A$ is at height $h$ from point $B$. The particle slides along the frictionless surface. When the particle reaches point $B$, its angular momentum about $\mathrm{O}$ will be: (Take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ )

Two rotating bodies $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ of masses $\mathrm{m}$ and $2 \mathrm{~m}$ with moments of inertia $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{I}_B\left(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}\gt\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}}\right)$ have equal kinetic energy of rotation. If $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{L}_B$ be their angular momenta respectively, then

A particle is projected at time $t=o$ from a point $P$ on the ground with a speed $V_o$ at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. What is the magnitude of the angular momentum of the particle about $\mathrm{P}$ at timet $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{o}} / \mathrm{g}$

The diameter of a solid disc is $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ and its mass is $16 \mathrm{~kg}$. What torque is required to increase its angular velocity about an axis perpendicular to its plane from zero to 120 rotations/minute in 8 seconds?

A string is wrapped around the rim of a wheel of moment of inertia $0.20 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2$ and radius $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. The wheel is free to rotate about its axis initially the wheel is rest. The string is now pulled by a force of $20 \mathrm{~N}$. The angular velocity of the string after 5 seconds will be :
